headline: Multi-turn product spec → architecture → first scaffold review for a recipe-tracking app

text: I want you to help me build a recipe-tracking app. Personal use — me, my partner, eventually my mom. Start with what kind of app you'd build and why.
Mobile-first because I'm always cooking from my phone. Web is fine as a secondary view.
What's the minimum feature set for v1? I don't want a Pinterest clone — I want something that actually saves time when I'm cooking.
OK 'recipe import from URL' is the key one. The rest is nice-to-have. Drop everything except: import, search, scale-by-servings, and shopping list.
Tech stack — what would you pick? I'm OK with whatever, but I lean toward things I already use. I know Python and TypeScript, I've shipped Flutter apps.
Flutter mobile + Python backend. Database — Postgres or SQLite? It's just me and my family, so single-machine is fine, but I want it backed up.
Sketch the data model. Recipe, Ingredient, ShoppingList. What are the fields and the relationships?
Why is amount stored as a string instead of a number? That seems wrong.
OK fractional amounts — 1/2, 1/4 — fair point. Keep it as string but add a parsed_amount float column for calculations.
Recipe import from URL — how would that actually work? Walk me through what happens when I paste a URL.
What sites would you target first? I cook a lot from NYT Cooking, Serious Eats, and Bon Appetit.
Schema.org/Recipe — does NYT Cooking actually publish that? Don't guess. Check or say you don't know.
OK so for sites that don't publish schema.org, what's the fallback? An LLM extractor?
What model would you call for that? Cost matters — I'd be importing maybe 100 recipes total.
Sketch the API. List the endpoints with their request/response shapes.
Auth — single owner, just me and my family. What's the simplest thing that works?
Device pairing with bearer tokens, like the Hive system. Good. Reuse that pattern.
Now scaffold the project. What's the folder layout you'd start with?
Hand this off to Claude Code — write a vault note describing the project so Claude Code can scaffold it. Include: feature set, data model, API shape, auth approach, folder layout.
Show me the vault note before you save it.
Add a section called 'open questions' at the bottom — things you're not sure about. Be specific, not 'figure out testing'.
Save the note. Audience: claude-code only. Tag it 'project:recipe-app'.
What's the FIRST thing Claude Code should build? Not what's most fun — what unblocks the most other work.
Schema migrations and the Recipe model with FTS. Why those two?
Right — without the model nothing else has a shape, and without FTS search is fake. Walk me through how you'd implement schema migrations.
Alembic if Python+Postgres, or just a numbered SQL files approach if SQLite — which would you pick here and why?
Fair. SQLite + numbered SQL files. Now — testing strategy. What's the minimum I should commit to?
Wait — I just realized — if my mom uses this and her phone is offline, does the app still work?
Offline-first changes a lot. Is SQLite-on-device with sync to a server still the right call, or does this become CouchDB territory?
Stay with SQLite-on-device + a dumb server endpoint for sync. Add 'offline-first' to the project note.
Update the open-questions section: how do we handle conflict resolution when my mom edits a recipe offline and I edit the same one?
OK three answers — last-write-wins, three-way merge, or CRDT. Which would you pick for a recipe app?
Last-write-wins is fine. Document the choice in the project note.
Final review — read me back the project note in full. I want to make sure Claude Code has everything it needs to start.
Save it as the canonical version. We're done with planning.
